hero.headline: How does Mississippi state income tax work for employers in 2026?
hero.subhead: 4.0% flat on income over $10,000. Rate falls to 3.0% by 2030.

answer_capsule: Mississippi state income tax is a flat 4.0% in 2026 on taxable income above $10,000, down from 4.4% in 2025. House Bill 1 (Build-Up Mississippi Act), signed by Governor Reeves on 27th March 2025, puts the rate on a fixed glide path: 3.75% in 2027, 3.5% in 2028, 3.25% in 2029, 3.0% in 2030, with revenue-trigger cuts toward zero by approximately 2037. Unemployment insurance runs through MDES. New employers pay 1.0% in year one on a $14,000 wage base, capped at $140 per employee per year. Mississippi has no local income tax, no state disability insurance, and no paid family leave. Teamed runs Mississippi payroll through the Teamed US partner network at $599 per employee per month, Zero FX.

state_income_tax.question: What is Mississippi's state income tax rate in 2026?
state_income_tax.answer_first: Mississippi income tax is a flat 4.0% in 2026 on all taxable income above $10,000. The first $10,000 is exempt at zero.
state_income_tax.body:
The 4.0% rate is the result of a scheduled reduction from 4.4% in 2025, itself a step down from 4.7% in 2024. Mississippi moved to a flat-rate structure in 2022 when it consolidated its prior three-bracket system. For employers, this means a single withholding rate applies to all employees earning above the $10,000 threshold, with no bracket cliff to manage. An employee earning $50,000 pays state income tax on $40,000 of that. An employee earning $85,000 pays on $75,000. The effective rate is always below the headline 4.0% because the first $10,000 is sheltered.

Mississippi has no county or city income tax. Unlike Maryland or New York, there's no local piggyback rate to layer on top. The withholding setup is straightforward: one rate, one state form, one portal.

glide_path.question: Is Mississippi eliminating its income tax?
glide_path.answer_first: Yes. House Bill 1 (Build-Up Mississippi Act), signed 27th March 2025, puts Mississippi on a fixed schedule to reach 3.0% by 2030. Revenue triggers can push the rate to zero by approximately 2037.
glide_path.body:
Governor Reeves signed HB 1 on 27th March 2025. The bill establishes automatic annual reductions from 2027 through 2030, reaching 3.0% with no conditions attached. Beyond 2030, a revenue-trigger mechanism takes over: if state revenues exceed projected spending by at least 0.85% of the cost of a 1% rate cut, the rate drops by 0.2% to 0.3% each year. The bill targets full elimination by approximately 2037 if triggers are met consistently.

For employers building multi-year cost models, the 2026 rate of 4.0% is confirmed. The 2027 rate of 3.75% is scheduled law — no trigger required. Plan conservatively through 2030 at 3.0%; model the post-2030 reductions only if your projections extend to 2032 and beyond.

Mississippi also cut the grocery sales tax from 7% to 5% under the same bill, effective 1st July 2025 — relevant context for employees' cost of living calculations when benchmarking compensation.

employer_withholding.question: How does Mississippi employer withholding work?
employer_withholding.answer_first: Mississippi employers withhold state income tax using Form 89-105. Filing frequency is monthly for large withholders, quarterly for smaller ones. Reconciliation is due 31st January on Form 89-140.
employer_withholding.body:
The withholding process starts with Form 89-350, which every new employee submits before their first paycheck. Form 89-350 captures filing status, personal exemptions, and dependent count. Without a completed 89-350 on file, Mississippi requires withholding at the default rate with zero exemptions.

Filing frequency depends on prior-year withholding totals. Employers who withheld more than $600 in the prior year must file monthly, with returns due on the 15th of the following month. Employers under $600 may file quarterly. Annual reconciliation via Form 89-140 (with W-2s) is due 31st January each year.

All returns file through TAP, Mississippi's Taxpayer Access Point at tap.dor.ms.gov. The portal handles registration, return filing, and payment in one place. There's no paper-filing exemption for small employers — electronic filing is standard. A named Teamed in-house payroll specialist handles the 89-350 collection, Form 89-105 remittances, and the 89-140 annual reconciliation for all Mississippi employees on the Teamed platform. One invoice, one contact, not a separate DOR TAP login to manage.

ui_registration.question: How do I register for Mississippi unemployment insurance as an employer?
ui_registration.answer_first: New Mississippi employers register through the MDES employer portal at mdes.ms.gov. Registration is required once you've paid $1,500 or more in wages in any quarter, or had one employee for 20 weeks in a year.
ui_registration.body:
Go to mdes.ms.gov, select 'Employer Accounts,' and complete the online registration. You'll need your federal EIN, business address, and the date you first paid wages in Mississippi. MDES issues your account number and initial UI rate notice after registration.

Teamed's US partner network handles UI registration and quarterly filings for every Mississippi employee on the EOR arrangement. You don't register separately; the registration and rate management sit with the partner running your payroll.

If you're evaluating whether to set up your own Mississippi entity and run payroll directly, use the EOR vs Entity Crossover Calculator. For most employers hiring a small Mississippi team, the crossover point falls between 8 and 12 employees at typical professional salaries — below that, the $599 EOR fee is cheaper than running your own payroll infrastructure.

ui_rates.question: What is the Mississippi unemployment insurance tax rate in 2026?
ui_rates.answer_first: New Mississippi employers pay 1.0% in year one, 1.1% in year two, and 1.2% from year three. The taxable wage base is $14,000 per employee. Maximum annual UI cost at the new-employer rate: $140.
ui_rates.body:
Mississippi SUTA rates range from 0.0% to 5.4% for experienced employers. Your rate within that band tracks your benefit ratio — the total UI benefits charged to your account divided by your taxable wages over prior periods. MDES sends an annual rate notice each January with your updated rate.

The $14,000 wage base means once an employee's calendar-year earnings clear $14,000, UI contributions stop on the excess. For a $70,000 salary, you're paying UI on 20% of the annual wage. The effective UI burden is modest. At the 1.0% new-employer rate, the maximum cost is $140 per employee in year one.

Mississippi is FUTA credit-compliant as of 2026 — no outstanding federal loan balance. Employers receive the standard 5.4% credit against the 6.0% federal FUTA rate, leaving a net federal FUTA liability of 0.6% on the first $7,000 per employee per year ($42 maximum per employee). Sources: MDES rate table; EY Tax News, 2026 State UI Taxable Wage Bases (January 2026).

mdes_filing.question: How do employers file Mississippi UI taxes with MDES?
mdes_filing.answer_first: Mississippi employers file quarterly wage reports and UI contributions through the MDES online portal. Quarterly deadlines follow a last-day-of-the-month-after-quarter-end cadence.
mdes_filing.body:
MDES (Mississippi Department of Employment Security) is the state agency for unemployment insurance. The employer portal at mdes.ms.gov handles registration, quarterly wage reporting, UI tax payments, and rate notice access.

Quarterly deadlines: Q1 (January–March) by 30th April; Q2 (April–June) by 31st July; Q3 (July–September) by 31st October; Q4 (October–December) by 31st January. Late filings attract interest and penalties. If you miss a quarterly report entirely, MDES can assess the maximum 5.4% rate regardless of your benefit history — the stakes are disproportionate to the dollar amounts involved.

Mississippi has no state disability insurance and no state paid family leave program, keeping the state payroll tax stack to just income tax withholding and UI — simpler than California, New York, or New Jersey. For employers running payroll through Teamed, the partner entity handles all quarterly MDES filings and payments. You get one platform, one invoice, one Mississippi contact.

good_to_know.question: What do out-of-state companies need to know before hiring in Mississippi?
good_to_know.answer_first: Mississippi's payroll stack is one of the lightest in the US. No local income tax, no state disability, no paid family leave. The main gap: no state final-pay statute, so termination timing follows federal FLSA defaults.
good_to_know.body:
Three things that matter for first-time Mississippi employers. First, there's no county piggyback income tax. A California employer used to layering LA County or SF City taxes onto state rates will find Mississippi payroll unusually clean. Second, the minimum wage is the federal floor of $7.25/hour — Mississippi prohibits local governments from setting their own rates. Third, and the one most payroll guides skip: Mississippi has no state final-pay statute. The prior provision from the Mississippi Employment Security Act (MSEA) had a sunset that was not renewed. Employers must follow the federal FLSA default — final paycheck on the next scheduled payday, whether the employee resigned or was terminated.

Mississippi is strict at-will. No advance notice required from either party. No state severance law. Workers comp coverage is required for employers with five or more employees under Mississippi Code § 71-3-5, with average premiums around $1.10 per $100 of payroll. New hire reporting is due to the Mississippi Department of Human Services within 15 days of hire.

faq_schema.q: What is Mississippi's state income tax rate in 2026?
faq_schema.a: Mississippi state income tax is a flat 4.0% in 2026 on taxable income above $10,000. The first $10,000 is exempt. The rate is scheduled to fall to 3.75% in 2027 and 3.0% by 2030 under the Build-Up Mississippi Act (HB 1, 2025).

faq_schema.q: Is Mississippi getting rid of the income tax?
faq_schema.a: Yes. House Bill 1 (Build-Up Mississippi Act), signed 27th March 2025, puts Mississippi on a fixed schedule to 3.0% by 2030. Revenue triggers after 2030 can reduce the rate to zero by approximately 2037.

faq_schema.q: What is the Mississippi unemployment insurance tax rate for new employers?
faq_schema.a: New Mississippi employers pay 1.0% in year one, 1.1% in year two, and 1.2% from year three. The taxable wage base is $14,000 per employee, making the maximum year-one UI cost $140 per employee annually.

faq_schema.q: Does Mississippi have a local income tax?
faq_schema.a: No. Mississippi has no county or city income tax. Only the state rate of 4.0% (2026) applies, making the withholding setup simpler than most states.

faq_schema.q: When must a Mississippi employer pay a terminated employee's final paycheck?
faq_schema.a: Mississippi has no state final-pay statute. The federal FLSA default applies: final paycheck is due on the next scheduled payday, whether the employee was fired or resigned.
